GWAS Attention Score Comparison

2026-02-06

Introduction

This document compares the old and new GWAS attention scores to assess changes in the methodology and their impact on disease prioritization.

Load Libraries

library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(data.table)
library(rineq)
library(ggplot2)

Load Data

We load both the new and old attention score datasets.

# Load the new dataset
gwas_attention_new <- fread(here("Data/merged_dataset_exclude_Injuries_2023.csv")) %>%
  select(cause_name = `Cause Name`, cause_id, total_attention_score) %>%
  filter(!duplicated(cause_id))

# Load the old dataset for comparison
gwas_attention_old <- fread(here("Data/Ncase_merged_dataset_exclude_Injuries.csv")) %>%
  select(cause_name = `Cause Name`, cause_id, total_attention_score) %>%
  filter(!duplicated(cause_id))

Merge Datasets

merged <- merge(gwas_attention_new, gwas_attention_old, by = "cause_id", suffixes = c("_new", "_old"))
merged
## Key: <cause_id>
##      cause_id                       cause_name_new total_attention_score_new
##         <int>                               <char>                     <num>
##   1:      297                         Tuberculosis                   1323388
##   2:      298                             HIV/AIDS                     12536
##   3:      300 HIV/AIDS resulting in other diseases                         0
##   4:      302                   Diarrheal diseases                         0
##   5:      319                        Typhoid fever                      8850
##  ---                                                                        
## 300:     1014                   Osteoarthritis hip                   6259960
## 301:     1015                  Osteoarthritis knee                  15785982
## 302:     1016                  Osteoarthritis hand                    768405
## 303:     1017                 Osteoarthritis other                    724644
## 304:     1048                             COVID-19                 407462440
##                            cause_name_old total_attention_score_old
##                                    <char>                     <num>
##   1:                         Tuberculosis                   1383542
##   2:                             HIV/AIDS                    184563
##   3: HIV/AIDS resulting in other diseases                    111873
##   4:                   Diarrheal diseases                     50868
##   5:                        Typhoid fever                      8850
##  ---                                                               
## 300:                   Osteoarthritis hip                   6259960
## 301:                  Osteoarthritis knee                  15785982
## 302:                  Osteoarthritis hand                    768405
## 303:                 Osteoarthritis other                    724644
## 304:                             COVID-19                 407462440

Correlation Analysis

Calculate the Pearson correlation between old and new attention scores:

cor(merged$total_attention_score_new, merged$total_attention_score_old, method = "pearson")
## [1] 0.997015

This is really high - but it’s because there are some really large values -

ggplot(merged, aes(x = total_attention_score_old, y = total_attention_score_new)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "blue") +
  labs(title = "Correlation between Old and New Attention Scores",
       x = "Old Attention Score",
       y = "New Attention Score") +
  theme_minimal()

Examining the relationship for smaller attention scores:

ggplot(merged, aes(x = total_attention_score_old, y = total_attention_score_new)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "blue") +
  labs(title = "Correlation between Old and New Attention Scores (Zoomed In)",
       x = "Old Attention Score",
       y = "New Attention Score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  xlim(0, 1e7) +
  ylim(0, 1e7)

There are a number of new attention scores that are zero but were previously non-zero - Identify diseases those diseases

sum(merged$total_attention_score_new == 0 & merged$total_attention_score_old > 0)
## [1] 59

List of studies that now have zero attention scores

merged %>%
  filter(total_attention_score_new == 0 & total_attention_score_old > 0) %>%
  select(cause_name_new, cause_id)
